assumptions: One-time hardware purchase (CapEx)
Monthly costs: electricity ($20/mo @ $0.12/kWh, 5 hours/day), cloud storage/backups ($10/mo), misc consumables ($5/mo)
No recurring cloud costs
Assumes used workstation to reduce initial cost
Servos can be purchased incrementally (start with 6, add more later)
Safety equipment essential for human-robot interaction
Workbench/tools assumed available (subtract $250 if you have a desk)

components.name: Intel RealSense D435 (minimal hardware for data collection)

assumptions: Minimal upfront cost - use cloud for heavy compute
Monthly cloud costs breakdown:
- AWS EC2 g5.2xlarge (NVIDIA A10G): $1.21/hour × 40 hours/mo = $48
- NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud (Isaac Sim): $100/mo subscription
- Storage (S3, datasets): $20/mo
- Data transfer egress: $15/mo
- Misc cloud services (Lambda, CloudWatch): $12/mo
Development happens in cloud simulation (Isaac Sim)
Deploy final models to Jetson for real-world testing
Assumes existing laptop with decent CPU (i5/i7 or equivalent)
No physical robot during development phase
Best for algorithm research before hardware commitment
Moderate upfront investment - local development + occasional cloud bursts
Monthly costs breakdown:
- Electricity ($25/mo, local workstation + Jetson usage)
- Cloud (spot instances for heavy training): $40/mo average
- NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud (periodic use): $25/mo (shared team account or pay-as-you-go)
Strategy: Develop locally on CPU/small models, burst to cloud for large training runs
Use cloud Isaac Sim for photorealistic simulation, Gazebo locally for iteration
Start with 6 servos (robotic arm), expand to 12-20 for humanoid later
Best balance of cost and capability for serious hobbyists/small teams
Workstation powerful enough for ROS 2 + Gazebo + TensorFlow training (small models)

components.name: Educational Kit Bundle (alternative)
components.note: Alternative: MyCobot 280 Edu Bundle includes Pi + servos + frame

assumptions: Ultra-budget setup for students/beginners
Monthly costs: electricity only ($10/mo), free cloud tier (Google Colab GPU)
Use free simulation: Gazebo on laptop, Google Colab for training
Hobby servos (SG90) are weak but sufficient for learning (0.18 Nm)
Limitations: Low torque (can't lift heavy objects), no force sensing, basic vision
Upgrade path: Replace hobby servos with Dynamixel XL430 one at a time ($50 each)
Best for: Learning ROS 2, basic computer vision, control algorithms
NOT suitable for: Production applications, heavy manipulation, outdoor use
Alternative bundle: MyCobot 280 Edu ($699) is turn-key but less customizable
